questions.prompt: Best approach to building a complex nested Auto Layout screen?

choices.text: Bottom-up. Build the smallest reusable components first (a row, a button, an avatar), verify each resizes correctly in isolation, then compose them into sections, then sections into screens. Top-down (start with screen frame, fill in details) typically leads to inner pieces needing rework when you realize the section layout doesn't accommodate them. Bottom-up means each layer is solid before the next builds on it; when you assemble a section from already-working components, the section composes correctly. When you assemble a screen from already-working sections, the screen works. Common pattern for production designs: build atomic components first (often into a Components page or library), then arrange them in screen layouts. This is also how engineering builds — small components composed up — so designs match implementation intent. Bonus: bottom-up components become library candidates; less rework as design system emerges. Top-down tends to produce ad-hoc layouts that don't reuse well.

questions.explanation: Bottom-up: smallest reusable components first → sections → screens. Top-down leads to rework when inner pieces don't fit.

questions.prompt: Card design needs a 'NEW' badge in the top-right corner overlapping content. Best Auto Layout approach?

choices.text: Add badge to Auto Layout flow.

Position the badge as 'absolute' within the card. In Figma: hold Alt while moving the badge OUT of Auto Layout flow → Figma marks it as 'absolutely positioned' relative to parent; it still belongs to the parent (moves when parent moves) but doesn't participate in the layout flow (siblings ignore it for spacing/positioning). Set the badge's position via x/y or constraints (top-right pinning). Now: badge floats over the card; card content flows normally; resizing the card moves the badge with it; badge doesn't push other elements. Adding the badge to Auto Layout flow would push other elements down/right, breaking the card's intended layout. The 'absolute within Auto Layout' is the Figma pattern for overlay decorations: badges, floating action buttons, status dots, etc. Use sparingly — most elements should flow with Auto Layout; absolute-position when the element is genuinely overlapping rather than flowing.

questions.explanation: Alt+drag out of flow = 'absolute position' within parent. Badge floats over content; doesn't push siblings.
